name: dynamic-forward
context: Create a SOCKS proxy on the client with ssh -D for ad hoc access through an SSH path
info: Dynamic forwarding makes SSH act as a SOCKS 4/5 proxy. Instead of predeclaring one destination, client software picks arbitrary destinations and the SSH client forwards them through the encrypted connection. Agents use it for browser-based testing, temporary egress through a bastion, and tooling that already understands SOCKS proxies.
triggers: create a socks proxy with ssh
body:
# Dynamic Forwarding

Dynamic forwarding creates a local SOCKS proxy that can reach arbitrary destinations through the SSH session.

```bash
ssh -D 1080 ops@bastion.example.com -N
```

Point SOCKS-aware tools at `127.0.0.1:1080` and let them choose destinations dynamically. This is useful when you do not know every endpoint ahead of time or when a browser, package manager, or debugger already supports SOCKS proxy settings.

sections.title: Use SSH as a SOCKS proxy
sections.body: `ssh -D port host -N` creates a SOCKS 4/5 listener on the client. Applications that understand SOCKS can then route traffic through the SSH path without additional destination-specific forwards. This is the flexible option when multiple internal services need temporary access.

sections.title: Use only with SOCKS-aware clients
sections.body: Dynamic forwarding is not transparent TCP redirection. The application must know how to speak SOCKS or be wrapped by tooling that does. For a single fixed service such as a database, [[ssh/tunnel/local-forward]] is simpler and easier to reason about.
